sections.heading: Orations 27–28: On Theology
sections.html:
Who May Theologize, and How

Gregory of Nazianzus opens his theological orations — delivered in Constantinople around 380, in the midst of intense Arian and Pneumatomachian controversy — with a pointed question about the conditions of legitimate theological speech. Not every person, not in every context, and not with every audience is theological discourse appropriate. Gregory identifies the preconditions: purity of soul (theology spoken from an unclean heart defiles the subject), genuine inquiry (not debate for show or controversy for social position), and a prepared audience. Theology is not the common property of the market or the theater: "To discuss theology is not for everyone, I tell you, not for everyone — it is no such inexpensive or effortless pursuit."

This prologue is not intellectual elitism. Gregory's target is the casual theological controversialism that had infected Constantinople's public life, where cab drivers and shopkeepers traded positions on the divine nature as entertainment. The problem is not that these people are uneducated; it is that they approach the living God as a topic rather than as a Person before whom one stands in reverence. The appropriate posture for the theologian is modeled by Moses, who could not see God's face but saw his back (Exodus 33:23) — meaning that direct, exhaustive comprehension of the divine essence is denied to creatures, while a genuine, partial, transforming knowledge is granted. Theology is always this: real but limited, genuine but humble.

The Incomprehensibility of God and Apophatic Theology

The Twenty-Eighth Oration is Gregory's sustained argument for divine incomprehensibility — the via negativa that shapes all subsequent Eastern Orthodox theology. Gregory's argument is not skepticism about theological language but a calibration of what that language can and cannot do. We can say what God is not with greater confidence than we can say what he is, because the divine nature infinitely exceeds all creaturely categories. When we say God is "without beginning," we are more certain than when we attempt any positive description of what his eternity is like from the inside.

Gregory presses this by examining the failure of even the greatest human minds to comprehend God. He does not deny that God can be known — the whole of Scripture assumes he can be. But the knowledge granted is always mediated, partial, and accommodated. Paul's word is decisive:

1 Corinthians 13:12 — "For now we see through a glass, darkly; but then face to face: now I know in part; but then shall I know even as also I am known."
The eschatological qualifier matters: full, unmediated knowledge of God is a promised future reality, not a present possession. The theologian who speaks as though he has already arrived at that knowledge speaks from pride, not from revelation. Apophatic theology — the discipline of knowing when to stop speaking — is not agnosticism but reverence.

Against Rash Speculation

Gregory's practical conclusion is a warning against the kind of theological speculation that goes beyond what Scripture warrants and what the analogy of faith requires. The Eunomians (radical Arians) claimed not merely that the Son was subordinate to the Father but that the divine essence was fully comprehensible — that to know God as Unbegotten was to know his entire nature. Gregory regards this as the opposite error to agnosticism: a presumptuous confidence that the human mind can exhaust the divine. Against this he cites Job's encounter with God from the whirlwind, where God's response to Job's demand for explanation is not an answer but a torrent of counter-questions about creation's depths that Job cannot plumb (Job 38:4).

The proper theological attitude is what Gregory calls "ordered boldness" — speaking what Scripture has revealed, with precision and care, while falling silent where Scripture is silent. The Nicene theologians are not timid about dogmatic definition; Gregory will defend the full deity of the Son and Spirit with great precision in the following orations. But the precision is in the service of the mystery, not a replacement for it. The council's language (homoousios — of the same substance) is a fence around the mystery, not a dissection of it.

sections.heading: Orations 29–30: On the Son
sections.html:
The Eternal Generation of the Son

The Twenty-Ninth Oration opens Gregory's positive Christological argument. The Son is eternally generated from the Father — not created at a point in time, not a subordinate being of lesser substance, but genuinely and fully God, sharing the one divine nature with the Father and the Spirit. Gregory grounds the eternal generation in the concept of the Father as the eternal Principle (arche) of the Godhead. The Father is unbegotten; the Son is eternally begotten of the Father; the Spirit eternally proceeds from the Father. These relations are real distinctions, but they are distinctions within the one divine being, not between three gods.

Gregory is precise about the language of "begetting." It does not imply time, because the generation is eternal — there was never a moment when the Son was not. It does not imply material division, as though the Father's substance were cut or diminished. It does not imply the inferiority of the generated to the generator, because the Son receives the whole of the divine nature from the Father, not a part. The closest analogy in human experience is the generation of thought from the mind — the thought is genuinely from the mind, shares its nature, and yet the mind is not diminished. But even this analogy breaks down:

Isaiah 53:8 — "Who shall declare his generation?"
The manner of the eternal generation remains finally beyond human comprehension; what theology can and must assert is that it is real and that it communicates the full divine nature.

Against the Arian Proof-Texts

The Thirtieth Oration is largely exegetical — a sustained engagement with the scriptural texts the Arians used to argue for the Son's inferiority. Gregory's method is the first systematic application of the two-natures hermeneutic to disputed Christological texts: statements that seem to imply limitation, ignorance, dependence, or subordination in the Son must be interpreted as referring to his humanity, not to his divinity. This is not special pleading but a corollary of the incarnation: if the Son truly became human, then texts that record him hungering, weeping, praying, suffering, and dying are recording genuine human experiences, not deficiencies of his divine nature.

Gregory works through the key texts. The Son's statement "the Father is greater than I" (John 14:28) refers to the Son's economic subordination in the state of humiliation, not to an eternal ontological inferiority. The Son's ignorance of the hour of his return (Mark 13:32) is referred to his human nature, in which he did not always exercise the divine omniscience he possessed. The cry of dereliction (Matthew 27:46) Gregory reads as the voice of our human estrangement, which Christ takes up and presents to the Father on our behalf. In each case the interpretive key is the same: the Word became flesh, and the flesh's limitations are real but do not compromise the Word's eternal, equal deity.

The Communicatio Idiomatum

Gregory's most sophisticated contribution in these orations is his development of the communicatio idiomatum — the communication of attributes. Because the one Person of the Son is both fully divine and fully human, the attributes and experiences proper to each nature can be predicated of the one Person. This is not a confusion of the natures but a recognition of their personal unity. Gregory can say that God was born of Mary, and that the Son of God was crucified — not because the divine nature was born or suffered in itself, but because the Person who was born and suffered is the divine Son.

Galatians 4:4 — "But when the fulness of the time was come, God sent forth his Son, made of a woman, made under the law."

The practical stakes of this doctrine are soteriological. If the one who died on the cross was not truly and fully God — if the Arians are right that he is a created being, however exalted — then the cross is the death of a creature, and its atoning value is finite and insufficient. Only a divine person can bear the weight of infinite sin; only the Son in whom "all the fulness of the Godhead" dwells bodily (Colossians 2:9) can be the propitiation for the sins of the world. Gregory's Trinitarian theology is never merely speculative; it is always in service of the gospel's claim that God himself, in the Son, has entered human history to save.

  • John 14:28
  • Mark 13:32
  • Matthew 27:46
  • Galatians 4:4
  • Colossians 2:9
  • Isaiah 53:8

sections.heading: Oration 31: On the Holy Spirit
sections.html:
The Full Deity of the Spirit

The Thirty-First Oration — Gregory's most celebrated — is the first extended dogmatic defense of the full deity of the Holy Spirit in Christian literature. The Pneumatomachians ("Spirit-fighters," also called Macedonians) accepted the full deity of the Son while denying it to the Spirit, holding that the Spirit was a creature — perhaps the highest creature, but a creature nonetheless. Gregory meets this position with the same logic he used against the Arians: a Spirit who is less than fully divine cannot sanctify, cannot deify, cannot unite the creature to God. What is merely creaturely cannot raise the creature to participation in the divine nature.

Gregory's positive argument draws on the Spirit's works. The Spirit moved over the face of the waters at creation (Genesis 1:2). The Spirit overshadowed Mary at the incarnation (Luke 1:35). The Spirit descended on Christ at his baptism (Matthew 3:16). The Spirit is the agent of the new birth without which no one enters the kingdom (John 3:5). The Spirit distributes gifts to the church for its edification (1 Corinthians 12:11). These are divine works — works of creation, incarnation, regeneration, and ongoing governance of the church. A creature does not perform such works; only God does. Therefore the Spirit is God.

Procession from the Father and the Trinitarian Grammar

Gregory distinguishes the Spirit's mode of origin from the Son's. The Son is begotten; the Spirit proceeds. Both originate from the Father as the sole Principle (arche) and Cause (aitia) within the Godhead, but by different modes of origin. Gregory is frank that the content of this distinction — what the difference between begetting and procession actually is — surpasses human understanding. He appeals to the dominical word:

John 15:26 — "But when the Comforter is come, whom I will send unto you from the Father, even the Spirit of truth, which proceedeth from the Father, he shall testify of me."
Scripture names the mode (procession) and the source (the Father); it does not explain the metaphysics of procession. Gregory is content to hold the scriptural datum without forcing it into a philosophical category.

Gregory's formulation here — the Spirit proceeding from the Father alone — stands as the Eastern Orthodox position, and it became the fault line between Eastern and Western Christianity when the Western church later inserted filioque ("and from the Son") into the Nicene Creed. Gregory himself does not address the filioque question, but his insistence that the Father is the sole Principle of the Godhead, and that the Spirit's procession is from the Father, became the exegetical and theological foundation for the Eastern rejection of the addition. Both East and West agree on the Spirit's full deity; they differ on the eternal relation between Spirit and Son.

Progressive Revelation and Why the Spirit Was Not Named God Explicitly in Scripture

Gregory's most historically significant argument in the Thirty-First Oration addresses a pressing objection: if the Spirit is fully God, why does Scripture never call him "God" with the directness that it calls the Father God and (in passages like John 1:1 and John 20:28) the Son God? Gregory's answer introduces the concept of progressive revelation as a theological principle. God has accommodated his self-disclosure to the capacity of his people at each stage of redemptive history. The Old Testament disclosed the Father with clarity while leaving the Son in relative shadow. The New Testament disclosed the Son fully and gloriously while leaving the full implications of the Spirit's deity to be drawn out rather than stated with the same directness.

Gregory explains this accommodation not as divine deception but as divine pedagogy. Israel, emerging from a polytheistic environment, was not ready to receive the full Trinitarian disclosure. Even the disciples at the time of Christ's ministry were not able to bear the full weight of the Spirit's revelation — hence Christ's word:

John 16:12–13 — "I have yet many things to say unto you, but ye cannot bear them now. Howbeit when he, the Spirit of truth, is come, he will guide you into all truth."
The Spirit's own coming after Pentecost is the vehicle of the revelation of his own deity. The church's reception of the full Trinitarian faith — Father, Son, and Spirit, one God in three Persons — is itself the Spirit's gift, completing in history what was only adumbrated in earlier revelation.

  • John 15:26
  • John 16:12–13
  • John 1:1
  • John 3:5
  • Genesis 1:2
  • Luke 1:35
  • 1 Corinthians 12:11
  • 1 Corinthians 13:12
